name: internal order q
statement_text:

                                                          b       .

t for the tableau
                                      c A

Stage i has ‘internal order q’, if for all trees such that r(t) ≤ q,
                                                                r(t)
                                                               ci
                                              Φi (t) =              .
                                                               γ(t)

The significance of this definition is that if stage i has internal order q, then,
in any step with initial value yn−1 = y(xn−1 ), the value computed in stage
i satisfies Yi = y(xn−1 + hci ) + O(hq+1 ). Note that the C(q) condition is

necessary and sufficient for every stage to have internal order q, and this is
possible only for implicit methods.
  We are now in a position to generalize the remarks we have made about
third and fourth order methods.
statement_latex:
\[
\text{Definition 323A}\quad
\text{Consider a Runge–Kutta method given by the tableau}
\]
\[
\left[\begin{array}{cc}
c & A \\
  & b^T
\end{array}\right].
\]
For a tree \(t\) and stage \(i\), let \(\Phi_i(t)\) denote the elementary weight associated with \(t\) for the tableau
\[
\left[\begin{array}{cc}
c & A \\
e_i & A
\end{array}\right].
\]
Stage \(i\) has ‘internal order \(q\)’, if for all trees such that \(r(t) \le q\),
\[
\Phi_i(t) = \frac{c_i^{r(t)}}{\gamma(t)}.
\]
The significance of this definition is that if stage \(i\) has internal order \(q\), then, in any step with initial value \(y_{n-1} = y(x_{n-1})\), the value computed in stage \(i\) satisfies \(Y_i = y(x_{n-1} + hc_i) + O(h^{q+1})\). Note that the \(C(q)\) condition is necessary and sufficient for every stage to have internal order \(q\), and this is possible only for implicit methods.

We are now in a position to generalize the remarks we have made about third and fourth order methods.
context_latex:
\paragraph{Context}
Runge--Kutta method defined by tableau $c\; A / b^{\T}$ with abscissae $c=(c_i)$, coefficient matrix $A=(a_{ij})$ and weights $b=(b_i)$. For a rooted tree $t$, let $r(t)$ be its order, $\gamma(t)$ its density and $\Phi_i(t)$ the elementary weight for stage $i$. The condition $C(q)$ for stage $i$ is
\[
\Phi_i(t)=\frac{c_i^{r(t)}}{\gamma(t)}\quad\text{for all trees }t\text{ with }r(t)\le q.
\]
If $C(q)$ holds then the stage value satisfies $Y_i=y(x_0+c_i h)+O(h^{q+1})$.

variables.name: Runge-Kutta method
variables.definition: A method given by a tableau with c (vector of abscissae), A (matrix of coefficients), and b (vector of weights).

variables.name: tableau
variables.definition: A representation of a Runge-Kutta method as c A / b^T.

variables.name: c
variables.definition: Vector of abscissae (c_i) for the Runge-Kutta method.

variables.name: A
variables.definition: Matrix of coefficients (a_{ij}) for the Runge-Kutta method.

variables.name: b
variables.definition: Vector of weights (b_i) for the Runge-Kutta method.

variables.name: tree t
variables.definition: A rooted tree used in the theory of Runge-Kutta order conditions.

variables.name: Φ_i(t)
variables.definition: Elementary weight associated with tree t for stage i, defined for a specific tableau.

variables.name: r(t)
variables.definition: Order (or number of vertices) of the tree t.

variables.name: γ(t)
variables.definition: Density (or symmetry factor) of the tree t.

variables.name: C(q)
variables.definition: Condition on the Runge-Kutta coefficients ensuring certain order properties for the stages.

variables.name: Y_i
variables.definition: Value computed in stage i of the Runge-Kutta method.

variables.name: y(x)
variables.definition: Exact solution of the ODE being solved.

variables.name: h
variables.definition: Step size in the numerical method.

variables.name: e_i
variables.definition: Standard basis vector with 1 in the i-th position, used to modify the tableau.

preamble: The text discusses order conditions for Runge-Kutta methods, using rooted trees and elementary weights. The definition introduces the concept of 'internal order q' for a stage i, based on comparing elementary weights to a specific formula involving the abscissa c_i and tree properties. The significance is that internal order q implies the stage approximates the exact solution at a shifted argument to order q+1.

dependencies.name: The Taylor expansion of the approximate solution
